actions.target_entity: Robinhood Crypto; Uniswap Labs; ConsenSys (MetaMask); Kraken (Payward Inc.); Cumberland DRW — SEC Gensler-era enforcement withdrawals (2025)
actions.penalty_note: No monetary penalties on any dismissal. Kraken dismissal was with prejudice (bars re-filing identical charges). ConsenSys/MetaMask and Cumberland dismissals were without prejudice. Robinhood and Uniswap were investigation closures (no formal charges filed, no court order). Coinbase (27 Feb 2025) and Binance (29 May 2025) tracked separately. SEC filed only 456 total enforcement actions in 2025 — lowest since 2006.
actions.summary: Between February and April 2025, the SEC under Acting Chair Mark Uyeda and subsequently confirmed Chair Paul Atkins (Senate-confirmed 21 April 2025) systematically withdrew or closed Gensler-era crypto enforcement actions. Beyond the Coinbase and Binance dismissals tracked separately, the wave included: Robinhood Crypto — SEC investigation closed without charges, 21 February 2025; Uniswap Labs — Wells notice investigation closed without action, 25 February 2025; ConsenSys / MetaMask — formal dismissal stipulation filed 27 March 2025; Kraken (Payward Inc.) — formal dismissal with prejudice filed 27 March 2025; and Cumberland DRW — dismissal filed 27 March 2025. The SEC filed only 456 total enforcement actions in 2025, the lowest count since 2006. Atkins characterised the shift as redirecting resources toward high-harm fraud and market manipulation rather than novel securities theories applied to crypto infrastructure. Kraken's with-prejudice dismissal provides the strongest legal protection; investigation closures (Robinhood, Uniswap) carry no formal prejudice designation and can theoretically be reopened under a future administration.

sources.label: SEC litigation release — Kraken dismissal with prejudice (lr-26278)
SEC press release — original Kraken charges (Nov 2023)
Uniswap Labs blog — SEC investigation closed (25 Feb 2025)
Robinhood newsroom — SEC closes investigation (21 Feb 2025)

lessons: Gensler → Atkins transition produced the first administration-driven wholesale reversal of a crypto enforcement posture — shifting SEC from regulation-by-enforcement (novel securities theories against exchanges and wallets) to rulemaking dialogue; the same tokens and business models that were sued in 2023 face a fundamentally different regulatory environment in 2025.

Kraken's with-prejudice dismissal is meaningfully stronger protection than the without-prejudice dismissals: it bars the SEC from refiling the identical claims, while ConsenSys's without-prejudice filing leaves theoretical refiling options open under future administrations.

Investigation closures (Robinhood, Uniswap) are discretionary and carry no formal prejudice — RWA platforms should distinguish between investigation closures and court-filed dismissals when assessing residual regulatory risk from the 2025 retreat.

actions.summary: On 15 October 2021, the CFTC settled with Tether Operations Limited and its parent iFinex Inc., finding that Tether had falsely represented USDT as fully backed by US dollar reserves at all times. The $42.5M settlement comprised a $41M civil penalty against Tether and a $1.5M penalty against iFinex. The CFTC found that between 2016 and 2019, Tether maintained sufficient fiat reserves for only 27.6% of the days it claimed full backing, and that reserves were at times held in commingled accounts with Bitfinex, an affiliated exchange. Earlier in February 2021, Tether and iFinex had separately settled New York Attorney General charges for $18.5M, agreeing to provide periodic reserve transparency reports. The October 2021 CFTC action was the first federal enforcement specifically targeting stablecoin reserve misrepresentations and set the legal baseline for subsequent disclosure requirements.
lessons: Stablecoin reserve claims are material representations under commodity law: "fully backed by USD" must hold continuously, not merely at attestation snapshots.
CFTC asserted jurisdiction over stablecoin reserves via the swap/commodity hook, predating any dedicated US stablecoin statute.

actions.penalty_note: No monetary penalty; NYDFS ordered halt to BUSD issuance
actions.summary: On 13 February 2023, NYDFS issued a directive ordering Paxos Trust Company to cease minting new Binance USD (BUSD) tokens. At the time, BUSD was the third-largest stablecoin with approximately $16 billion in circulation. BUSD operated under a co-branding arrangement: Paxos issued and held reserves for the tokens while Binance distributed them under its brand. NYDFS cited unresolved supervisory concerns about Paxos's oversight of the Binance relationship — the regulator explicitly affirmed that BUSD reserves were fully backed and that the order was not about reserve adequacy. Paxos continued to honour redemptions on all outstanding BUSD under its New York trust charter. By early 2024, BUSD supply had declined to near zero as holders redeemed. No monetary penalty was imposed on Paxos. The action demonstrated that NYDFS can effectively wind down a multi-billion-dollar stablecoin through a minting halt alone.
lessons: State-level NYDFS can shut down a multi-billion-dollar stablecoin without federal SEC action — BUSD wind-down completed without any monetary penalty.
BitLicense scope strictly bounds what a NY-regulated issuer may issue; activity outside scope triggers a consent order to halt.

actions.penalty_note: $4.47B settlement (June 2024): $3.586B disgorgement + $420M civil penalty + ~$467M prejudgment interest. Do Kwon extradited Montenegro→US Dec 2024.
actions.summary: On 16 February 2023, the SEC sued Terraform Labs and co-founder Do Kwon for securities fraud arising from the May 2022 collapse of the TerraUSD (UST) algorithmic stablecoin, which wiped approximately $40 billion in market value within days. The SEC alleged that Terraform misled investors about UST's price-stability mechanism and that Anchor Protocol's 20% annual yield — presented as organic market returns — was artificially subsidised from Terraform's own reserves. Following a jury verdict in the SEC's favour, Judge Jed Rakoff (SDNY) entered final judgment on 12 June 2024 requiring Terraform Labs to pay $4.47 billion in disgorgement and civil penalties, and Do Kwon personally to pay approximately $204 million. Do Kwon, who had evaded multiple jurisdictions since the collapse, was arrested in Montenegro and extradited to the United States in December 2024. The case is the largest fraud judgment in US crypto enforcement history.
lessons: Algorithmic stablecoins without exogenous collateral are structurally pre-disposed to death-spiral failure; SEC framed UST/LUNA as securities fraud by design, not merely a bad outcome.
Issuer false statements about reserve mechanics constitute securities fraud regardless of the "stablecoin" label.
Cross-jurisdictional founder accountability is achievable: extradition obtained Montenegro→US after $4.47B civil settlement.

actions.penalty_note: 9-year ban on directors from MAS-regulated activities
actions.summary: On 13 September 2023, MAS reprimanded Three Arrows Capital (3AC) Pte Ltd and issued nine-year prohibition orders barring co-founders Zhu Su and Kyle Davies from carrying out regulated activities under Singapore law. MAS found that 3AC — registered as a fund management company — had managed assets exceeding the S$250 million AUM threshold applicable to registered managers without upgrading to a Capital Markets Services licence, and had provided materially false information to MAS regarding its AUM. 3AC collapsed in June 2022 after suffering heavy losses on Terra/Luna and other leveraged positions, with estimated liabilities exceeding USD 3 billion at the time of insolvency; Singapore courts appointed liquidators, with parallel proceedings in the British Virgin Islands. The co-founders departed Singapore prior to the collapse and have not submitted to local jurisdiction. This entry covers the September 2023 MAS regulatory findings against the corporate entity and its founders.
lessons: MAS escalation pattern: reprimand followed by 9-year prohibition orders on founders — personal accountability survives corporate insolvency.
3AC 2022 collapse triggered contagion across stablecoin/lending sector (BlockFi, Celsius, Voyager); non-RWA event with system-wide RWA-adjacent impact.

actions.penalty_note: Enhanced licensing conditions imposed, not a punitive penalty
actions.summary: In August 2023, HashKey Exchange and OSL Digital Securities became the first two virtual asset trading platforms (VATPs) in Hong Kong to receive SFC licences permitting retail customer access, operating under the regulatory framework formalised on 1 June 2023. Both platforms were licensed under Type 1 (dealing in securities) and Type 7 (providing automated trading services). SFC imposed standardised licensing conditions governing retail client onboarding: mandatory Know-Your-Customer procedures, suitability assessments, and standardised risk disclosure documents prior to account activation. Minimum liquid capital thresholds were imposed to ensure ongoing financial soundness. Retail clients may only access SFC-approved spot virtual assets; tokenised securities and over-the-counter derivative products are restricted to professional investors. These conditions are not a punitive enforcement action but the operational baseline required for any VATP seeking retail-tier access in Hong Kong, and function as the sector's de facto reference standard for custody, disclosure, and client categorisation.
lessons: HK VATP regime functions as ex-ante licensing rather than ex-post enforcement — distinct from the US "regulation by enforcement" template.
HashKey and OSL are the reference data points for any HK RWA tokenization platform seeking retail customer access; the licensing conditions are the de facto operational specification.

actions.penalty_note: $50M to SEC + parallel $50M to 32 state securities regulators coordinated via NASAA. First-of-its-kind action framing a retail crypto interest-bearing account as an unregistered security.
actions.summary: On 14 February 2022 the SEC settled charges against BlockFi Lending LLC over its BlockFi Interest Accounts (BIAs), finding that the BIAs were unregistered securities under the Securities Act of 1933 and that BlockFi itself failed to register as an investment company under the Investment Company Act of 1940. The SEC further found BlockFi had made misleading statements about the risk profile of its loan portfolio. BlockFi paid a $50M SEC civil penalty plus a parallel $50M to 32 state regulators (NASAA-coordinated) and committed to bring its products into compliance within 60 days. This was the first SEC enforcement to treat a retail crypto interest account as a security and remains the canonical precedent applied to subsequent yield-product cases (Genesis/Gemini Earn, Celsius, Voyager) and — by direct analogy — to RWA-tokenised yield distribution products.
lessons: Howey test applies to crypto yield programs regardless of "lending" or "interest account" branding — what matters is whether returns depend on the platform's managerial efforts, not the product name.

BlockFi precedent now actively framed against RWA-tokenised yield distribution: any RWA platform that pools investor capital and promises returns from issuer-managed allocation faces the same securities-law theory.
Parallel federal + multi-state enforcement via NASAA doubled the effective penalty and signalled that state securities regulators will pursue crypto issuers independently of SEC timing.

actions.target_entity: JPEX (Japan Easy Exchange) and associated promoters
actions.penalty_note: No SFC monetary penalty; case escalated to HK Police criminal proceedings under AMLO. As of March 2026: 26 individuals formally charged (conspiracy to defraud, money laundering, obstruction of justice), 80+ arrests, Interpol Red Notices on 3 fugitive ringleaders, HK$228M (~US$28M) in assets frozen.
actions.summary: On 13 September 2023 the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission issued a public alert naming JPEX (Japan Easy Exchange) as an unlicensed virtual asset trading platform making misleading claims about its regulatory status. Within days JPEX raised withdrawal fees to 999 USDT (capped at 1,000 USDT) effectively freezing user assets. Hong Kong Police launched Operation Iron Gate; by early 2026 more than 2,700 victims had reported losses exceeding HK$1.6 billion (~US$206M), making this the largest cryptocurrency fraud in Hong Kong history. As of March 2026, 26 individuals — including KOL promoters Joseph Lam Chok and Chan Wing-yee — have been formally charged under AMLO with conspiracy to defraud, money laundering, and obstruction of justice; three alleged ringleaders remain at large under Interpol Red Notice. The case directly catalysed HK's accelerated VATP licensing regime, the SFC's suspicious-VA-platforms public alert list, and KOL/influencer accountability rules for crypto promotion.
lessons: JPEX is the foundational case shaping HK's 2024–2026 RWA regulatory posture: the SFC public-alert mechanism, accelerated VATP licensing transition, and KOL accountability rules all trace directly to this collapse.

Unlicensed VATP custody is a single-point-of-failure: users had no on-chain self-custody fallback once withdrawal fees were weaponised, illustrating why any HK RWA tokenisation platform must satisfy ex-ante Type 1/7 licensing rather than relying on ex-post enforcement.

HK prosecutes unlicensed-platform operators and promoters under AMLO §53ZRG rather than dedicated securities/VATP statutes — a precedent any RWA tokenisation marketing campaign in HK must internalise.

actions.target_entity: Hounax (unlicensed virtual asset trading platform, HK-targeted)
actions.penalty_note: No SFC monetary penalty. HK Police Commercial Crime Bureau received 131 victim reports of losses totalling HK$120M (~US$15.4M). At SFC's request, HK Police blocked Hounax websites and removed social media pages. As of June 2026, no arrests have been publicly confirmed.
actions.summary: On 1 November 2023, the SFC added Hounax to its Suspicious Virtual Asset Trading Platforms list after the platform made false claims about business partnerships with named financial institutions and venture capital firms. By late November 2023, 131 victims had reported losses totalling HK$120 million (approximately US$15.4 million) to HK Police's Commercial Crime Bureau; investors described being lured via social media and WhatsApp group chats promoting investment hot tips, before finding themselves unable to withdraw assets. At the SFC's request, HK Police blocked access to Hounax's websites and social media pages. As of June 2026, no arrests have been publicly confirmed. Hounax represents the second major HK crypto fraud alert issued within two months of the JPEX collapse (September 2023), exploiting investor confidence during the gap period before the June 2024 mandatory VATP licensing deadline. The case contributed to accelerating SFC enforcement posture on unlicensed VATP operations and to the HK Legislative Council's push for tighter platform accountability rules.

sources.label: SFC Suspicious VATP alert — Hounax (1 Nov 2023)
SCMP: HK Police vow arrests after Hounax fraud (Nov 2023)

lessons: The JPEX collapse (September 2023) created an investor-confidence vacuum that fraudulent platforms like Hounax exploited within weeks; the SFC alert-list mechanism combined with police website-blocking is the primary first-response tool against unlicensed platforms without a clear HK nexus for formal VATP proceedings.

Hounax underscores the HK enforcement gap pre-June 2024: the non-contravention period allowed unlicensed platforms to operate and defraud investors before the mandatory VATP licensing deadline took effect, concentrating fraud risk precisely during the regulatory transition window.

Post-JPEX, the HK enforcement pattern is consistent — SFC public alert → police investigation → website block → prolonged criminal proceedings; RWA tokenisation platforms must distinguish this ex-post consumer-protection track from the ex-ante SFC VATP licensing regime, which provides no retrospective protection to defrauded investors of unlicensed platforms.

actions.penalty_note: Wells notice only. SEC formally terminated the investigation on 9 July 2024 with no enforcement action (Tenreiro termination letter). Separate from the parallel NYDFS halt order on the same product.
actions.summary: On 3 February 2023 the SEC issued a Wells notice to Paxos Trust Company alleging that the Binance USD (BUSD) stablecoin — issued by Paxos in partnership with Binance — was an unregistered security. Paxos publicly disputed the framing, arguing BUSD was a USD-backed payment stablecoin fully reserved 1:1 with short-term dollar-denominated assets and therefore not an investment contract. After ~17 months of submissions, and following adjacent court rulings (Ripple programmatic-sale Jul 2023; SEC v. Binance BUSD-claim dismissal Jun 2024), SEC Enforcement staff formally terminated the investigation on 9 July 2024 with no charges filed. The termination is the canonical precedent that a properly-reserved fiat-backed payment stablecoin will not be pursued as an unregistered security at the US federal level. The SEC's July 2024 closure of the investigation was conveyed via a private staff termination letter (the "Tenreiro letter") and was not publicly released; on-record references are therefore limited to Paxos's own announcements.
lessons: Wells-notice termination after a sustained Wells submission process is itself precedent — Paxos established that a properly-reserved USD-backed payment stablecoin will not be charged as an unregistered security at the federal level.

Parallel federal (SEC) and state (NYDFS) regulators acted asymmetrically on the same product: NYDFS halted BUSD issuance in 2023 while SEC ultimately declined to charge — venue selection materially shapes the issuer's outcome.

Adjacent court rulings (Ripple Jul 2023; Binance BUSD-claim dismissal Jun 2024) cumulatively shifted SEC enforcement willingness on stablecoins, illustrating how unrelated cases set the doctrinal backdrop for active investigations.

Regulatory signal velocity: the combined SEC Wells notice (Feb 3) and NYDFS halt order (Feb 13) triggered a ~50% decline in BUSD market cap — from ~$16B to ~$8B — within 60 days, with no conviction and no monetary penalty against Paxos. Regulatory intent alone, without adjudication, can effectively terminate a stablecoin.

actions.target_entity: Genesis Global Capital LLC and Gemini Trust Company LLC
actions.penalty_note: SEC complaint filed Jan 12, 2023. Genesis settled with SEC for $21M (Aug 2024) as a bankruptcy administrative claim. Gemini Earn user obligations resolved via Genesis bankruptcy ($2.18B in-kind distributions by Jun 2024) + separate NYDFS consent order ($37M, Feb 2024).
actions.summary: On 12 January 2023 the SEC sued Genesis Global Capital LLC and Gemini Trust Company LLC in SDNY alleging the Gemini Earn programme — launched Feb 2021, under which Gemini customers loaned crypto assets to Genesis in exchange for interest — was an unregistered securities offering raising over $900M from US retail investors. Genesis filed Chapter 11 eight days later (20 Jan 2023). The case was the SEC's first major action framing a yield product operated as a partnership between an exchange (Gemini) and a separate lender (Genesis) as a single unregistered securities offering with joint liability. Genesis settled with the SEC for $21M as a bankruptcy administrative claim in Aug 2024; Earn customers ultimately received full in-kind recovery through the Genesis bankruptcy plus an NYDFS-coordinated $37M penalty and $40M bankruptcy contribution from Gemini.
lessons: The SEC framed Earn as a joint offering: an exchange partnering with a separate lender to distribute a yield product does not insulate either party from securities-law liability — both Gemini and Genesis were named.
Bankruptcy timing was decisive: Genesis Chapter 11 followed the SEC charges by 8 days; recovery for Earn customers came through bankruptcy distribution rather than SEC penalty mechanics.
Multi-regulator stack (SEC + NYDFS + NYAG) on a single yield product — RWA platforms with yield distribution should anticipate the same stacked enforcement geometry.

actions.penalty_note: $37M penalty to NYDFS + $40M contribution to Genesis Global Capital bankruptcy estate for Earn customers. Gemini committed to return at least $1.1B in digital assets (actual return: $2.18B in-kind by Jun 2024). Gemini retained its DFS-issued trust charter.
actions.summary: On 28 February 2024 NYDFS entered a consent order with Gemini Trust Company resolving allegations that Gemini failed to conduct adequate due diligence on its Earn programme partner Genesis Global Capital — an unregulated third party — and failed to maintain compliance practices commensurate with the safety and soundness of a NY trust company. Under the order Gemini committed to return at least $1.1B in digital assets to Earn customers via the Genesis bankruptcy (final distributions completed June 2024 totalling $2.18B in-kind), paid a $37M penalty to NYDFS, and contributed $40M to the Genesis Global Capital bankruptcy estate. Gemini retained its DFS trust charter — contrasting with NYDFS's January 2024 action against Genesis Global Trading which required surrender of its BitLicense.
lessons: NYDFS treats counterparty due diligence as a charter-level safety-and-soundness obligation: outsourcing a yield product to an unregulated third party (Genesis) without adequate oversight is itself the violation, distinct from federal securities-law theories.

State-level remediation achieves customer recovery that federal enforcement cannot: NYDFS-coordinated bankruptcy contribution + in-kind digital-asset return materially outperformed pure civil penalty mechanisms.

Charter preservation as calibrated leverage: allowing Gemini to retain its trust charter while pulling Genesis Global Trading's BitLicense demonstrates NYDFS proportionality based on each entity's role and remediation posture.

sources.label: NYDFS consumer alert — Earn customer recovery details

actions.target_entity: Celsius Network LLC and Alex Mashinsky
actions.penalty_note: Four-regulator simultaneous action 13 Jul 2023: FTC $4.7B settlement (judgment suspended for bankruptcy distribution) + permanent ban on handling consumer assets; SEC + CFTC civil charges; DOJ criminal charges. Mashinsky pleaded guilty 3 Dec 2024 (commodities fraud + scheme to manipulate CEL token); sentenced 8 May 2025 by Judge Koeltl (SDNY) to 12 years prison + $48.4M forfeiture.
actions.summary: On 13 July 2023 the SEC, CFTC, FTC, and DOJ simultaneously announced parallel actions against Celsius Network and its founder/CEO Alex Mashinsky. The FTC reached a $4.7B settlement (suspended for bankruptcy distribution) plus a permanent ban on Celsius handling consumer assets; SEC and CFTC alleged securities and commodities fraud; DOJ filed criminal charges including securities fraud, commodities fraud, and wire fraud. Prosecutors argued Mashinsky misled investors about the safety and profitability of Celsius's yield-generating platform, used customer deposits to manipulate the CEL token price, and personally extracted ~$48M while ~$20B in customer deposits were locked. Mashinsky pleaded guilty 3 Dec 2024 to commodities fraud and CEL token manipulation, and on 8 May 2025 was sentenced to 12 years in federal prison plus $48.4M forfeiture — among the longest crypto-related criminal sentences alongside SBF.
lessons: Four-regulator stacking (SEC + CFTC + FTC + DOJ) on a single yield product produced the largest civil penalty in FTC crypto history plus a 12-year criminal sentence — full-spectrum enforcement geometry that any RWA yield platform must internalise.

Personal accountability extends beyond corporate veil: Mashinsky's 12-year sentence and $48.4M forfeiture track tightly with token-price manipulation evidence, even when the corporate entity is in bankruptcy.

Token-price manipulation by an issuer is criminally prosecutable as commodities fraud (CFTC + DOJ) independently of any securities characterisation — broadens the prosecutable surface area significantly.

actions.target_entity: Voyager Digital LLC and Stephen Ehrlich
actions.penalty_note: FTC $1.65B judgment against Voyager corporate entities (suspended for bankruptcy distribution) + permanent ban on marketing or handling crypto consumer products. CFTC filed parallel fraud + registration failure charges against Ehrlich. Ehrlich personal FTC settlement finalised June 2025: $2.8M payment + lifetime crypto consumer-business ban (Ehrlich + spouse Francine Ehrlich as relief defendant).
actions.summary: On 12 October 2023 the FTC announced a settlement with Voyager Digital LLC and parallel charges against former CEO Stephen Ehrlich for falsely marketing customer accounts as FDIC-insured. The FTC complaint alleged that from 2018 through Voyager's July 2022 Chapter 11 bankruptcy, Voyager promised customer deposits — including conversions to USDC stablecoin — were 'as safe as at a bank' and 'FDIC INSURED', when in fact most funds were not FDIC-insured and customers lost over $1B when Voyager collapsed. The FTC entered a $1.65B judgment against the corporate defendants (suspended pending bankruptcy distribution) plus a permanent ban on marketing or handling crypto consumer products; the CFTC filed parallel fraud charges against Ehrlich. Ehrlich personally settled with the FTC in June 2025 for $2.8M plus a lifetime ban on crypto-related consumer business.
lessons: False FDIC-insurance claims on stablecoin and crypto-deposit products are FTC-actionable consumer deception independent of any securities-law theory — directly applicable to any RWA platform marketing USD-equivalence to retail users.
Voyager expands the regulator stack on yield platforms: FTC (consumer protection) joined SEC/CFTC/DOJ as a credible primary venue, with the $1.65B headline exceeding most SEC penalties.

Permanent industry ban as remedy: the FTC's permanent prohibition on both the corporate defendants and Ehrlich establishes a structural exit from the crypto consumer market that no civil monetary penalty alone achieves.

sources.label: FTC press release (Ehrlich individual settlement, Jun 2025)

actions.penalty_note: No MAS monetary penalty. Hodlnaut withdrew its Major Payment Institution (MPI) licence application Aug 2022; Singapore Police Commercial Affairs Department opened fraud investigation Nov 2022; Singapore High Court ordered liquidation 10 Nov 2023 (~S$92M / US$67M net liabilities).
actions.summary: On 8 August 2022 Hodlnaut Pte Ltd — a Singapore-based crypto lending platform — suspended customer withdrawals, deposits, and token swaps. On 13 August 2022 it applied to be placed under interim judicial management (granted 29 Aug 2022). Hodlnaut subsequently withdrew its Major Payment Institution licence application from MAS, conceding it could not operate as a regulated digital payment token provider. The Singapore Police Force's Commercial Affairs Department opened a fraud investigation in November 2022 into alleged misrepresentations about exposure to TerraUSD/Luna — judicial managers ultimately reported ~US$190M in TerraUSD losses, with US$187M of that booked through the Hong Kong subsidiary. On 10 November 2023 the Singapore High Court ordered the company wound up. Hodlnaut is the Singapore equivalent of the BlockFi/Celsius/Voyager wave and a foundational case for MAS's subsequent tightening of DPT licensing and consumer-protection rules. MAS did not issue a dedicated public statement on Hodlnaut in 2022; authoritative on-record references are limited to Singapore court filings, the principal judgment being 2023 SGHC 323 (winding-up order, 10 Nov 2023).
lessons: MPI licence withdrawal under regulatory pressure is functionally equivalent to enforcement: voluntary withdrawal closed MAS consumer-protection oversight precisely when customers needed it most, exposing a structural gap in the DPT licensing perimeter.

Cross-border subsidiary risk: ~98% of Hodlnaut's TerraUSD exposure was booked through its Hong Kong subsidiary, illustrating that Singapore-licensed parents can route platform risk into less-regulated APAC subsidiaries — a pattern RWA tokenisation platforms with multi-entity APAC structures must address.
MAS's 2023–2024 DPT consumer protection rules (retail leverage limits, custody segregation, marketing restrictions) trace directly to the Hodlnaut/3AC cluster.

actions.target_entity: Binance Holdings Ltd and Changpeng Zhao (CZ)
actions.penalty_note: $4.316B combined federal penalties: DOJ ~$1.81B (criminal fines + forfeiture for BSA, sanctions, unlicensed money transmitter); CFTC ~$1.35B; FinCEN/OFAC components (with offsets against DOJ). CZ personally pleaded guilty 21 Nov 2023 to BSA violation, paid $50M personal fine; sentenced 30 Apr 2024 to 4 months prison.
actions.summary: On 21 November 2023 the DOJ, CFTC, FinCEN, and OFAC simultaneously announced the largest crypto enforcement action to date: Binance Holdings pleaded guilty to operating an unlicensed money transmitter, conspiracy to violate the Bank Secrecy Act, and conspiracy to violate sanctions law (transactions for users in Iran, Cuba, Syria, North Korea, and Crimea). Combined federal penalties totalled ~$4.316B. Binance accepted a five-year DOJ monitorship and committed to a full exit from US users. Founder Changpeng Zhao (CZ) personally pleaded guilty to a BSA violation, paid a $50M personal fine, stepped down as CEO, and on 30 April 2024 was sentenced to four months in federal prison — substantially below Guidelines, reflecting cooperation. This action did not address Binance's separate SEC unregistered-securities case (dismissed 2025) but established BSA/AML/sanctions compliance as the dominant US theory against global crypto exchanges.
lessons: BSA/AML/sanctions compliance is the binding US theory against global exchange infrastructure, independent of securities-law characterisation — critical for any RWA settlement infrastructure that bridges US and sanctioned jurisdictions.

Five-year DOJ monitorship is structurally more consequential than the $4.3B headline: it embeds federal oversight inside the exchange's operations and shapes RWA-relevant custody and settlement architecture across that horizon.

Founder accountability via BSA: CZ's 4-month sentence (below Guidelines) signals that exchange founders who cooperate face short custodial sentences for AML failures — distinct from the multi-year sentences for fraud (Mashinsky 12y, SBF 25y).

actions.target_entity: Binance Holdings Ltd, BAM Trading Services (Binance.US), and Changpeng Zhao
actions.penalty_note: SEC voluntarily dismissed the case 29 May 2025 without monetary penalty. Distinct from the $4.316B DOJ/CFTC/Treasury settlement of November 2023, which remains in force.
actions.summary: On 5 June 2023 the SEC filed 13 charges against Binance Holdings, BAM Trading Services (Binance.US operator), and Changpeng Zhao alleging operation of an unregistered exchange, broker, and clearing agency; unregistered offer and sale of securities including BNB and BUSD; and misrepresentation of trading controls. The complaint named twelve tokens as unregistered securities (BNB, BUSD, SOL, ADA, MATIC, FIL, ATOM, SAND, MANA, ALGO, AXS, COTI), extending prior token-by-token enforcement to a wholesale-exchange theory. In June 2024 Judge Berman Jackson (DDC) dismissed certain BUSD-related claims relying on the Ripple programmatic-sale ruling. On 29 May 2025 the SEC under post-Gensler leadership voluntarily dismissed the case in full without prejudice and without monetary penalty. Together with the parallel Coinbase dismissal Feb 2025, marks the receding of exchange-level securities enforcement as the primary US theory against crypto market infrastructure.
lessons: The SEC's exchange-level securities theory was abandoned in 2025 without a single binding settlement against a major exchange — the dismissals (Coinbase Feb 2025, Binance May 2025, Robinhood 2025) collectively are a major precedent against the SEC's wholesale-exchange framing.

Court dismissals of BUSD claims (Jun 2024) extending the Ripple programmatic-sale doctrine narrowed the securities-law surface for stablecoin secondary-market trading — directly relevant to any tokenised-security platform's secondary-market design.
Voluntary dismissal without prejudice leaves a future SEC the technical option to refile — RWA platforms should not treat the 2025 retreat as a permanent safe harbour.

actions.target_entity: Coinbase Inc. and Coinbase Global Inc.
actions.penalty_note: SEC voluntarily dismissed the case 27 Feb 2025 without monetary penalty.
actions.summary: On 6 June 2023 — one day after the Binance complaint — the SEC sued Coinbase Inc. and Coinbase Global Inc. in SDNY alleging Coinbase operated as an unregistered national securities exchange, broker, and clearing agency, and that its staking-as-a-service programme was an unregistered securities offering. The complaint named tokens including SOL, ADA, MATIC, FIL, SAND, AXS, CHZ, FLOW, ICP, NEAR, VGX, DASH, and NEXO as unregistered securities. In March 2024 Judge Failla refused to dismiss most claims, validating the SEC's 'investment contract' application to secondary-market tokens — a significant intermediate doctrinal ruling. On 27 February 2025 the SEC under post-Gensler leadership voluntarily dismissed the case in full. The dismissal — preceding the Binance dismissal by three months — was the first major institutional signal of the SEC's withdrawal from exchange-level securities enforcement.
lessons: Staking-as-a-service was framed under the same Howey theory as BlockFi yield products, treating user-pooled staking rewards as managed-return securities — the theory survived motion-to-dismiss (Mar 2024) before SEC voluntarily abandoned the case (Feb 2025).

The Coinbase ruling preserving most SEC claims (Mar 2024) is the SEC's strongest doctrinal win on the exchange theory; abandoning it in Feb 2025 leaves the doctrine alive in case law without operational enforcement — complex precedent for RWA platforms to navigate.

Dismissal sequencing (Coinbase Feb 2025 → Binance May 2025) signalled deliberate policy retreat rather than case-specific weakness; RWA platforms with secondary-market tokenised-asset trading should track which doctrinal theories the SEC retains versus abandons.

actions.target_entity: Samuel Bankman-Fried (FTX Trading Ltd / Alameda Research)
actions.penalty_note: SDNY jury convicted on all seven counts (wire fraud, conspiracy to commit wire fraud, conspiracy to commit securities/commodities fraud, conspiracy to commit money laundering) 2 Nov 2023. Sentenced 28 Mar 2024 to 25 years prison + ~$11.02B forfeiture. Co-conspirators Caroline Ellison, Gary Wang, Nishad Singh, Ryan Salame all pleaded guilty separately (Ellison 24mo, Salame 7.5yr). Parallel SEC + CFTC civil actions resolved through bankruptcy.
actions.summary: Following the November 2022 collapse of FTX Trading Ltd, on 2 November 2023 an SDNY jury convicted founder Samuel Bankman-Fried on all seven criminal counts including wire fraud against FTX customers, FTX investors, and Alameda Research lenders; conspiracy to commit money laundering; and conspiracy to commit securities and commodities fraud. Evidence centred on the secret use of FTX customer deposits as a slush fund for Alameda's trading and venture investments. On 28 March 2024 Judge Lewis Kaplan sentenced Bankman-Fried to 25 years in federal prison plus an $11.02B forfeiture — the longest crypto-related criminal sentence to date. Co-conspirators Ellison (Alameda CEO), Wang (FTX CTO), Singh (FTX Director of Engineering), and Salame (FTX Digital Markets Bahamas) all pleaded guilty and cooperated; Ellison received 24 months, Salame 7.5 years. The case is the modern reference precedent for crypto-exchange customer-asset misappropriation and reshaped US and Bahamian regulatory posture toward exchange custody segregation.
lessons: Customer-asset commingling — using customer deposits as a corporate slush fund — is the most prosecutable failure mode for any custodial RWA platform; SBF's 25-year sentence is the benchmark, far above any tokenisation-related civil penalty.

Proof-of-reserves was the missing structural primitive: any RWA tokenisation platform with custody responsibilities must publish reserve attestations that materially exceed what FTX represented internally versus externally.

Co-conspirator cooperation pricing: Ellison/Wang/Singh/Salame guilty pleas yielded sentences 2–10 years, vs SBF's 25 years for taking trial — a clear DOJ incentive structure shaping how future crypto-fraud cases will resolve.

actions.target_entity: Ripple Labs Inc., Christian Larsen, and Bradley Garlinghouse
actions.penalty_note: Final judgment 7 Aug 2024: $125M civil penalty against Ripple. SEC originally sought ~$2B. Initial complaint Dec 22, 2020. Summary judgment 13 Jul 2023 (Torres, SDNY): XRP institutional sales WERE unregistered securities; XRP programmatic exchange sales were NOT. SEC dropped its appeal in 2025.
actions.summary: Filed by the SEC on 22 December 2020, SEC v. Ripple Labs alleged that Ripple, CEO Garlinghouse, and co-founder Larsen conducted an unregistered securities offering by selling XRP tokens. On 13 July 2023 Judge Analisa Torres (SDNY) issued a landmark summary judgment: XRP sold to sophisticated institutional buyers under written contracts WAS an unregistered security; XRP sold through programmatic trading on digital-asset exchanges to anonymous retail buyers was NOT a security, because retail buyers could not reasonably have expected returns to depend on Ripple's specific managerial efforts. This was the first US court ruling to bifurcate a token's securities status by sale context, breaking the SEC's binary framing. On 7 August 2024 Judge Torres entered final judgment requiring Ripple to pay a $125M civil penalty (vs the SEC's requested ~$2B). The SEC dropped its appeal in 2025. The Ripple programmatic-sale doctrine has since been cited in BUSD-claim dismissals (SEC v. Binance, Jun 2024) and the broader 2024–2025 SEC retreat from secondary-market token enforcement.
lessons: The Torres bifurcation (institutional sale = security; programmatic secondary-market sale ≠ security) is the most-cited US precedent for any RWA-tokenisation platform planning primary issuance versus secondary-market trading — different sales channels carry different legal characterisations of the same token.
The $125M final judgment vs the SEC's requested $2B (~94% reduction) signalled judicial skepticism of inflated SEC penalty asks and recalibrated future negotiation baselines for token issuers.

Ripple's programmatic-sale doctrine has been load-bearing in adjacent rulings (BUSD claims dismissed Jun 2024; Coinbase staking theory survived but case dismissed Feb 2025) — RWA platform legal strategy should track which downstream rulings extend or limit Torres's reasoning.

actions.target_entity: OFAC — Tornado Cash immutable smart contract SDN designations (vacated on appeal by Joseph Van Loon et al.)
actions.penalty_note: No monetary penalty; this entry records a judicial reversal of a regulator action, not a regulator-initiated enforcement. OFAC delisted Tornado Cash smart contract addresses from SDN list on 21 March 2025 following the 5th Circuit ruling. Separate criminal proceedings against co-developer Roman Storm (DOJ, SDNY) remain pending as of June 2026.
actions.summary: On 26 November 2024, the US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit unanimously reversed the district court in Van Loon et al. v. Department of the Treasury, No. 23-50669, holding that OFAC exceeded its statutory authority under IEEPA when it sanctioned Tornado Cash's immutable smart contracts in August 2022. The panel found that the smart contracts — autonomous, self-executing code that no party can modify, own, or exclude others from using — do not constitute 'property' for IEEPA purposes and therefore cannot be sanctioned. Applying the post-Loper Bright framework, the court declined to defer to OFAC's statutory interpretation. The ruling was remanded with instructions to grant plaintiffs' partial motion for summary judgment. Following the decision, OFAC delisted the Tornado Cash smart contract addresses from its SDN list on 21 March 2025, formally ending the sanctions regime on the protocol layer. This is the first US federal appellate decision holding that open-source immutable smart contract code falls outside the property-based reach of US economic sanctions — a structural constraint on OFAC's authority over protocol-layer DeFi infrastructure.

sources.label: 5th Circuit opinion — Van Loon v. Treasury, No. 23-50669 (26 Nov 2024)
Treasury press release — Tornado Cash delisting (21 Mar 2025)

lessons: Immutable smart contracts are not 'property' under IEEPA — sanctions cannot attach to autonomous on-chain code that no party owns or controls, regardless of whether sanctioned actors use it; OFAC must identify human operators or specific transactions rather than the protocol itself.

The ruling structurally constrains the sanctions toolkit for DeFi infrastructure: any RWA protocol that uses autonomous on-chain primitives (AMMs, escrow contracts, settlement rails) now has circuit-court authority that such code cannot itself be sanctioned.

Criminal prosecution of individual developers remains a distinct and parallel track — the 5th Circuit ruling does not insulate co-developers from DOJ wire fraud or money laundering charges; the Van Loon case and the Roman Storm SDNY prosecution coexist as separate theories.

actions.penalty_note: Rodriguez sentenced to 5 years' imprisonment and a $250,000 fine (6 November 2025, Judge Denise L. Cote, SDNY); Hill sentenced to 4 years and $250,000 fine (19 November 2025); both subject to 3 years of supervised release. Forfeiture judgment of $237,832,360.55 (total traceable criminal proceeds through the service); defendants satisfied this by paying $6,367,139.69 representing Samourai's actual earned transaction fees — the balance was uncollectable. penalty_usd reflects combined court-ordered fines ($250K × 2); forfeiture detail noted separately. Count 1 (conspiracy to commit money laundering) was dismissed pursuant to plea agreements; convictions on Count 2 only (conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money transmitting business, 18 U.S.C. § 1960). Each defendant also ordered to serve 3 years of supervised release. Hill was arrested in Portugal at the US government's request.
actions.summary: On 24 April 2024, the DOJ's SDNY office arrested Keonne Rodriguez (37, Harmony, Pennsylvania) and William Lonergan Hill (67, arrested in Portugal) — co-founders of Samourai Wallet, a Bitcoin privacy wallet offering Whirlpool CoinJoin mixing and Ricochet obfuscation services — on two counts: conspiracy to commit money laundering (max 20 years) and conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money transmitting business (max 5 years). Prosecutors alleged the service processed over $2 billion in total transactions and facilitated more than $100 million tied to darknet markets, with at least $237 million in traceable criminal proceeds. The FBI seized the samouraiwallet.com domain concurrently with the arrests. On 29–30 July 2025 both defendants pleaded guilty to Count 2 only; Count 1 (money laundering conspiracy) was dismissed. Rodriguez was sentenced to 5 years on 6 November 2025 and Hill to 4 years on 19 November 2025, each with a $250,000 fine. Defendants forfeited $6.37 million in earned fees against a $237.8 million forfeiture judgment.

sources.label: DOJ SDNY press release — Samourai founders arrested and charged (24 Apr 2024)
DOJ SDNY press release — Samourai founders plead guilty (Jul 2025)
DOJ SDNY press release — Samourai founders sentenced (Nov 2025)

lessons: Guilty pleas on the unlicensed money transmitting count (Count 2) while money laundering conspiracy (Count 1) was dismissed mirrors the partial Storm verdict in the Tornado Cash prosecution — prosecutors appear to face higher proof burdens on money laundering mens rea than on the regulatory registration requirement under 18 U.S.C. § 1960 for mixing-service operators.

The DOJ's theory — that operating a non-custodial Bitcoin wallet with mixing features constitutes an unlicensed money transmitting business — was validated by both guilty pleas, establishing that 'software only / non-custodial' arguments do not insulate developers who can be shown to have knowledge of criminal usage.

FBI's simultaneous seizure of samouraiwallet.com during arrest demonstrates that DNS infrastructure is a primary enforcement lever against privacy tools independent of open-source code availability — domain registrars and hosting providers are actionable chokepoints even when the underlying protocol code is freely mirrored.

actions.penalty_note: No monetary penalty at indictment. August 2025 trial result: Storm convicted on Count 3 (conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money transmitting business, 18 U.S.C. § 1960, max 5 years); sentencing pending retrial outcome. Jury deadlocked on Count 1 (conspiracy to commit money laundering, 18 U.S.C. § 1956, max 20 years) and Count 2 (conspiracy to violate IEEPA/sanctions, 50 U.S.C. § 1705, max 20 years). DOJ filed for retrial on Counts 1–2 on 9 March 2026, proposing October 5 or 12, 2026. Roman Semenov remains a fugitive and individually listed on OFAC's SDN list as of June 2026. See related entries: ofac-tornado-cash-sanctions-2022 and van-loon-v-treasury-tornado-cash-2024.
actions.summary: On 23 August 2023, the DOJ's SDNY office unsealed a federal indictment charging Tornado Cash co-founders Roman Storm (34, Auburn, Washington) and Roman Semenov (35, Russian national) on three counts: conspiracy to commit money laundering (max 20 years), conspiracy to violate IEEPA via sanctions evasion (max 20 years), and — for Storm only — conspiracy to operate an unlicensed money transmitting business (max 5 years). Prosecutors alleged the defendants knowingly laundered over $1 billion in criminal proceeds, including Lazarus Group funds, while making false public representations of sanctions compliance. OFAC concurrently added Semenov individually to its SDN list; he remains at large. Storm stood trial in July–August 2025 before Judge Polk Failla (SDNY). The jury convicted Storm on Count 3 (unlicensed money transmitting) but deadlocked on the money laundering and sanctions conspiracy counts. DOJ filed for retrial on the deadlocked counts on 9 March 2026, proposing an October 2026 start date. The case is the lead US prosecution testing criminal developer liability for open-source privacy-enhancing DeFi infrastructure.

sources.label: DOJ SDNY press release — Tornado Cash founders indicted (23 Aug 2023)
IRS Criminal Investigation — Storm convicted on Count 3 (Aug 2025)
CoinDesk — DOJ requests October 2026 retrial for Roman Storm (10 Mar 2026)

lessons: The jury's conviction on the unlicensed money transmitting count (Count 3) but deadlock on money laundering (Count 1) signals that courts apply significantly higher scrutiny to the mens rea element of money laundering when the defendant is a protocol developer rather than a direct participant in criminal transactions — a key risk variable for RWA infrastructure teams deploying programmable settlement rails.

Vacatur of the parallel OFAC smart-contract sanction (Van Loon, Nov 2024) provided no protection to individual developers: the criminal prosecution rests on personal intent-based theories entirely independent of the IEEPA property-law analysis rejected by the Fifth Circuit.

Semenov's individual SDN listing — unlike the protocol-level smart contract designations — was not disturbed by the Fifth Circuit ruling, confirming OFAC retains authority to designate identifiable human actors in DeFi networks regardless of whether the underlying protocol code is legally sanctionable.

actions.penalty_note: No monetary penalty; action designates Tornado Cash to the SDN list under IEEPA. OFAC delisted all Tornado Cash smart contract addresses from the SDN list on 21 March 2025 following the Fifth Circuit's ruling in Van Loon v. Department of the Treasury (26 Nov 2024) that immutable smart contracts do not constitute 'property' of a foreign national under IEEPA. Roman Semenov was added to the SDN list individually on 23 August 2023 (concurrent with DOJ indictment) and remains individually listed as of June 2026. See related entries: doj-tornado-cash-devs-2023 and van-loon-v-treasury-tornado-cash-2024.
actions.summary: On 8 August 2022, OFAC designated Tornado Cash — a decentralised, non-custodial Ethereum privacy protocol — as a Specially Designated National, adding approximately 44 Ethereum and USDC addresses to its SDN list. OFAC alleged the protocol had facilitated laundering of more than $7 billion in virtual currency since 2019, including over $455 million stolen by North Korea's Lazarus Group from the Ronin Network and Harmony Horizon Bridge hacks. Within hours Circle blacklisted the sanctioned wallets, freezing approximately $75,000 in USDC. This was the first time OFAC sanctioned immutable autonomous smart contract code as 'property' of a foreign national under IEEPA. That legal theory was unanimously rejected by the Fifth Circuit on 26 November 2024 in Van Loon v. Department of the Treasury. OFAC subsequently delisted Tornado Cash's smart contract addresses on 21 March 2025, formally ending the protocol-layer sanctions regime. Criminal proceedings against co-developer Roman Storm (DOJ, SDNY) continue on separate legal theories.

sources.label: Treasury press release — Tornado Cash SDN designation (JY0916)
The Block — Circle freezes USDC in Tornado Cash sanctioned wallets

lessons: OFAC's theory that immutable autonomous smart contracts constitute 'property' of their deployers under IEEPA was unanimously rejected by the Fifth Circuit — sanctions cannot attach to on-chain code that no party owns or controls post-deployment, a structural constraint now binding on OFAC's approach to protocol-layer DeFi infrastructure.

The Circle USDC freeze demonstrated that permissionless protocols retain centralised chokepoints: stablecoin blacklists were mobilised within hours of the SDN designation, before any court had ruled on legality — a compliance risk for any RWA protocol relying on USDC as a settlement or collateral asset.

OFAC sanctions and DOJ criminal prosecution are parallel tracks: the protocol-layer vacatur did not dismiss charges against individual developers; Roman Storm's SDNY prosecution proceeded to trial in 2025 under personal intent-based theories entirely distinct from the IEEPA property analysis.

actions.penalty_note: Default judgment also imposed permanent trading and registration bans and ordered protocol website shutdown and content removal. bZeroX LLC co-founders Tom Bean and Kyle Kistner settled separately for USD 250,000 on 22 September 2022 (concurrent CFTC order).
actions.summary: On 22 September 2022 the CFTC simultaneously settled with bZeroX LLC co-founders Tom Bean and Kyle Kistner for USD 250,000 and filed civil charges against Ooki DAO — the governance successor formed after bZeroX transferred protocol control to BZRX token holders, explicitly marketing the transfer as a mechanism to evade regulatory enforcement. On 8 June 2023, U.S. District Judge William H. Orrick (N.D. Cal.) entered a default judgment against Ooki DAO, imposing USD 643,542 in civil monetary penalties together with permanent trading and registration bans and an order to shut down all protocol-operated web infrastructure. The court held that Ooki DAO qualifies as an 'unincorporated association' under California law and a 'person' under the Commodity Exchange Act, establishing the first federal precedent that a decentralised token-holder governance collective does not confer immunity from regulatory jurisdiction. Underlying charges covered illegal off-exchange leveraged retail commodity transactions, operation as an unregistered Futures Commission Merchant, and failure to implement Bank Secrecy Act customer identification programs.
lessons: DAO governance structures do not confer regulatory immunity: Judge Orrick's unincorporated-association holding establishes that CFTC jurisdiction attaches to token-holder collectives as legal persons — the premise that protocol governance can be made 'enforcement-proof' via decentralisation is legally invalid under U.S. federal commodity law.

Founders retain liability after governance transfer: Bean and Kistner settled personally at the same moment the DAO was charged, demonstrating that handing off protocol control does not extinguish personal exposure for conduct that occurred during the prior operating phase.

Absence of a coordinated legal-defence mechanism is itself a material governance deficiency: Ooki DAO had no legal entity, no counsel, and no mechanism to respond to litigation — resulting in default judgment that established binding precedent with no adversarial challenge to the DAO's own interests.

sources.label: CFTC Press Release 8590-22 — bZeroX/Ooki DAO charges (22 Sep 2022)
CFTC Press Release 8715-23 — Default judgment statement (9 Jun 2023)

actions.penalty_note: No CFTC civil monetary judgment as of 2026-06 — action ongoing, seeking disgorgement, restitution, and civil penalties. Parallel DOJ criminal indictment (9 Jan 2023, SDNY) produced April 2024 jury convictions on commodities fraud, manipulation, and wire fraud; all criminal convictions vacated 23 May 2025 by Judge Arun Subramanian (Rule 29, venue and wire fraud materiality grounds) — not a merits acquittal. Eisenberg separately sentenced to 52 months (May 2025) on an unrelated conviction. In October 2022 Eisenberg returned approximately USD 67 million to the Mango Markets protocol treasury via a community governance vote, retaining an estimated USD 47 million.
actions.summary: On 9 January 2023 the CFTC filed a civil complaint — and the DOJ unsealed a parallel criminal indictment — against Avraham Eisenberg for an October 2022 oracle manipulation attack on Mango Markets, a Solana-based decentralised lending and trading protocol. Eisenberg opened two anonymous accounts, then purchased large quantities of MNGO tokens across FTX, Serum, and AscendEX, inflating the MNGO spot price approximately 13× in around 30 minutes and causing Mango's on-chain price oracle to reflect the elevated collateral value; he then borrowed over USD 110 million against the inflated collateral before returning roughly USD 67 million to the protocol treasury under a community governance vote. The CFTC characterised the scheme as commodity manipulation — its first civil enforcement action for manipulation on a decentralised digital asset exchange — and the case is the primary regulatory precedent for oracle-layer price manipulation as a commodity law violation. DOJ criminal convictions (April 2024) were vacated on procedural grounds in May 2025; the CFTC civil action remains ongoing.
lessons: Oracle price feeds are a primary attack surface in DeFi lending protocols: a single actor controlling cross-venue spot liquidity can manipulate an on-chain price oracle within minutes, bypassing smart-contract collateral logic that assumes price-feed integrity — TWAP, multi-source aggregation, and circuit-breaker design are RARM Layer 3 (oracle) prerequisites, not optional hardening.

Criminal conviction vacated on procedural grounds does not validate the underlying conduct: Judge Subramanian's Rule 29 dismissal turned on venue and wire fraud materiality, not a finding that oracle manipulation is lawful — the CFTC's civil characterisation of such conduct as commodity manipulation under the CEA remains the operative regulatory standard.

Parallel civil and criminal proceedings create asymmetric survival: CFTC civil actions seeking disgorgement and restitution are unaffected by criminal vacatur and continue to define the regulatory perimeter for DeFi manipulation, even where criminal prosecution collapses on procedural grounds.

sources.label: CFTC Press Release 8647-23 — Eisenberg civil complaint (9 Jan 2023)
CFTC Complaint PDF — Eisenberg oracle manipulation charges

actions.penalty_note: Penalty distributed to harmed investors via a Fair Fund. Entity-only settlement; no individuals charged. Galois announced fund liquidation February 2023 following the FTX collapse; approximately USD 40–45 million of ~USD 200 million AUM was trapped on FTX, representing roughly 50% of assets under management. Additional SEC finding: Galois permitted selective investor redemptions with as little as five days' notice, contrary to the fund's stated 30-day Limited Partnership Agreement term.
actions.summary: On 3 September 2024 the SEC settled with Galois Capital Management LLC — a now-defunct crypto-focused hedge fund — for USD 225,000, marking the first SEC enforcement action applying the Investment Advisers Act custody rule (Rule 206(4)-2) to digital assets held at a crypto exchange. From July 2022, Galois maintained client crypto-assets on FTX Trading Ltd. and Fireblocks; the SEC found that neither entity qualified as a 'qualified custodian' under the custody rule — FTX held only a South Dakota state trust licence, which does not satisfy the Rule 206(4)-2 qualified custodian standard. When FTX collapsed in November 2022, approximately USD 40–45 million of Galois's estimated USD 200 million AUM was inaccessible; the fund announced liquidation in February 2023 and sold its FTX bankruptcy claims at 16 cents on the dollar. The SEC also found that Galois permitted selective investor redemptions on as little as five days' notice, contrary to its stated 30-day Limited Partnership Agreement term. No individuals were charged; the civil penalty was distributed through a Fair Fund to harmed investors.
lessons: Exchange custody accounts are not qualified custodians under Rule 206(4)-2: the Galois action confirms that holding digital assets on a crypto trading platform — regardless of exchange scale or reputation — does not satisfy U.S. adviser custody requirements; RWA fund structures must segregate assets with a demonstrably qualified custodian (bank, registered broker-dealer, or qualifying FCM).

Custody non-compliance and counterparty concentration risk compound simultaneously: Galois's non-compliant custody arrangement meant that the regulatory violation and the catastrophic loss event were the same fact — assets held on FTX were both non-compliant and inaccessible when FTX failed, illustrating that custody compliance is a direct risk-management control, not merely an administrative requirement.

Entity-only settlement without personal charges does not reduce precedent weight: the SEC's first digital-asset custody enforcement establishes the applicable standard for all registered investment advisers managing crypto or tokenised asset funds — future fact patterns with similar custody failures may carry individual liability for fund managers who knew or should have known the custodian was unqualified.

sources.label: SEC Press Release 2024-111 — Galois Capital settlement (3 Sep 2024)
SEC Admin Proceeding IA-6670 — order and findings
Sidley Austin — First crypto custody rule enforcement analysis

actions.target_entity: Tron Foundation / BitTorrent Foundation / Rainberry Inc. / Justin Sun
actions.penalty_note: $10M civil penalty (Rainberry Inc. only, March 2026). Claims against Justin Sun, Tron Foundation Ltd., and BitTorrent Foundation Ltd. dismissed with prejudice; public terms for those parties not disclosed. 8 celebrity co-defendants settled separately.
actions.summary: On 22 March 2023 the SEC filed suit in SDNY (No. 1:23-cv-02433-ER) against Tron Foundation Limited, BitTorrent Foundation Ltd., Rainberry Inc., and founder Justin Sun, alleging that TRX and BTT tokens were sold as unregistered securities generating over $31.9M in illegal proceeds. The complaint also charged systematic wash trading — 609,790 confirmed trades averaging 2,449 daily through Sun-controlled nominee accounts — and coordinated secondary-market manipulation. Eight US celebrities were charged separately for undisclosed promotional payments: Lindsay Lohan, Jake Paul, Soulja Boy, Austin Mahone, Lil Yachty, Ne-Yo, Akon, and Kendra Lust. On 5 March 2026, the action resolved: claims against Sun, Tron Foundation, and BitTorrent Foundation were dismissed with prejudice; Rainberry Inc. agreed to pay a $10M civil penalty and accepted an injunction against future securities violations. Settlement terms for Sun and the foundations were not publicly disclosed.
lessons: Token distributions that fund issuer operations satisfy Howey even where tokens have concurrent blockchain utility — TRX/BTT held as securities despite Tron's live mainnet.
Undisclosed celebrity promotions for crypto tokens constitute unregistered touting under securities law; eight separate charges illustrate the breadth of liability.
Wash trading through nominee accounts exposes both issuer entities and their founders to parallel securities fraud and market manipulation charges.

actions.penalty_note: SEC initially sought ~$22.2M disgorgement. Following LBRY's insolvency the court-accepted judgment was substantially reduced; specific figure requires PACER docket verification (1:21-cv-00260-PB, D.N.H.). LBRY Inc. announced dissolution January 2023, citing inability to survive litigation costs.
actions.summary: On 29 March 2021 the SEC filed suit in the District of New Hampshire (No. 1:21-cv-00260-PB) against LBRY Inc., alleging LBRY Credits (LBC) were sold as unregistered securities to fund development of a decentralised content platform. On 7 November 2022 Judge Paul J. Barbadoro granted summary judgment for the SEC, holding that LBC satisfied the Howey test: investors purchased LBC in reasonable expectation of profits from LBRY's managerial efforts, regardless of LBC's concurrent utility as a content-tipping token. LBRY's core defence — that functional utility disqualified LBC as a security — was explicitly rejected. This ruling is a foundational precedent for the utility-token question: concurrent on-chain utility does not defeat Howey where the issuer retains and sells tokens to fund operations and investors reasonably expect returns from the issuer's efforts. In January 2023 LBRY Inc. announced dissolution, citing inability to survive litigation costs; Odysee, the consumer platform built on the LBRY protocol, continued operations under separate management.
lessons: Utility token defences fail under Howey where the issuer retains tokens and sells them to fund operations — functional use does not negate investment contract status.
SEC will pursue enforcement to issuer dissolution: company insolvency and small market cap do not confer immunity from securities-law registration requirements.
Protocol-layer resilience: LBRY Inc.'s dissolution did not terminate the LBRY protocol — Odysee continued, illustrating the separation of issuer entity from on-chain infrastructure.

actions.target_entity: Mek Global Limited / Peken Global Limited (KuCoin) / Chun Gan / Ke Tang
actions.penalty_note: DOJ: $112.9M criminal fine + $184.5M forfeiture = $297.4M. CFTC civil penalty: $500K. Founders Chun Gan and Ke Tang each forfeited $2.7M under two-year DPAs. Settlement announced 28 January 2025 (not December 2024 as sometimes reported). KuCoin (Peken Global Ltd.) pleaded guilty to one count of operating an unlicensed money transmitting business.
actions.summary: On 26 March 2024 the DOJ (SDNY) and CFTC filed coordinated actions against Mek Global Limited and related KuCoin entities — including Peken Global Limited (Turks and Caicos), PhoenixFin PTE Ltd., and Flashdot Limited — and against founders Chun 'Michael' Gan and Ke 'Eric' Tang. The DOJ charged KuCoin with operating as an unlicensed money transmitter and BSA/AML conspiracy, alleging facilitation of over $5B in suspicious transactions including darknet proceeds, ransomware payments, and sanctions evasion. In a parallel civil complaint, the CFTC charged KuCoin with operating as an unregistered futures commission merchant, swap execution facility, and introducing broker; the complaint explicitly classified Bitcoin, Ethereum, and Litecoin as commodities. On 28 January 2025, KuCoin (Peken Global) pleaded guilty and agreed to pay $297.4M in DOJ criminal fines and forfeiture; the CFTC imposed an additional $500K civil penalty. Founders Gan and Tang each entered two-year deferred prosecution agreements, forfeiting $2.7M each. KuCoin agreed to exit the US market for a minimum of two years.
lessons: Global exchanges accessible to US users face BSA/AML and CFTC commodity registration obligations regardless of offshore incorporation in Seychelles or Turks and Caicos.
Parallel DOJ criminal and CFTC civil proceedings compound liability: a single exchange can face both a criminal guilty plea and civil disgorgement simultaneously.
Founding executives face personal BSA liability through DPAs even when the corporate entity bears the guilty plea — $2.7M individual forfeitures plus two-year DPA each.
